original_reasoning: Arabic mutual pushing/jostling and Greek reciprocal receiving are two sides of an exchange/contact event.
حرقه keeps the sense of burning, singeing, or heat-driven intensity, which fits a caustic or burning-associated plant name.
Arabic gushing/streaming maps well to motion outward; Greek 'escape' is a clear abstract drift from flowing out.
Flax shreds and stitched material give a clear chain to something spread or stuffed into a pallet or cushion, στιβάδιον.
Arabic خمد means to sink, faint, or subside; Greek 'to the ground' preserves the same downward-settling concept.
Arabic means bulky and thick; bottle gourd strongly fits that same massy rounded concept.
Arabic 'arched/aquiline' clearly meets Greek 'helix' by the shared curved form.
Arabic worst lameness gives bodily crookedness or defective gait, and Greek is defective speech; this is a clear metaphorical chain from physical distortion to linguistic error.
